exemplar.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained focus on the prescribed title with clear definitions of key terms and framing of assumptions
Effective linkage to two Areas of Knowledge (Arts and Human Sciences) throughout the essay
Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by discipline-specific examples
Evidence of awareness and evaluation of different points of view
Coherent line of inquiry maintained in each section, repeatedly tying back to the central knowledge question
weaknesses: Evaluation is occasionally superficial; implications of arguments are noted rather than explored in depth
Some generalisations remain descriptive rather than analytically developed
Transitions between examples can feel abrupt, disrupting the essayâs flow
Opportunities to deepen the discussion of methodological differences and their bearing on knowledge fragility are not fully exploited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay consistently meets the descriptors for the 7â8 band: it maintains sustained focus on the title, links effectively to the chosen AOKs, and offers clear, supported arguments with counter-positions. However, uneven depth of evaluation and occasional superficial analysis preclude entry into the top band (9â10). Given the total mark of 7 set by the official examiner and the essayâs overall performance against the strand descriptors, a score of 7 accurately reflects its strengths and areas for improvement.

annotations.comment: The assumption that knowledge fragility and reverence are mutually exclusive is asserted without justification. The student should explain why these concepts cannot coexist or consider refining this limitation to strengthen the argument.

Excluding natural disasters as a cause of fragility seems ad hoc. The student should justify this exclusion or acknowledge how such external factors might nonetheless interact with methodological and ideological fragility.

The thesis in the final sentence of the introduction could be clearer. The student should explicitly state the central claim and preview the line of reasoning for both AOKs to guide the reader more effectively.
The student could strengthen this section by discussing why earlier theories remain influential despite being superseded, explaining how foundational knowledge continues to support new research.

The studentâs choice of Boorstinâs quotation effectively frames the essayâs theme by highlighting the risk of overconfidence in knowledge. This engages the reader and sets a clear tone for the exploration of fragility versus reverence.

The concept of âmaintenanceâ in the Human Sciences is introduced but not defined. The student should clarify what maintenance entails and how it systematically prevents fragility in theoretical knowledge.

The claim that human scientists have an âincentive to improve knowledgeâ would benefit from specifying the institutional or communal drivers (e.g., grant funding, peer review) that enforce this incentive.

The use of participant-observation ethnography as an example effectively illustrates how methodological rigor in anthropology underpins the stable transmission and reverence of knowledge within that discipline.

The student raises ethical concerns about applying Human Sciences knowledge but does not explore them. Adding an example of real-world harm would deepen the evaluation of fragility in practical contexts.

The suggestion that AI and technology could overcome fragility is promising but vague. The student should offer a concrete example of an AI tool or technological innovation that preserves or validates knowledge.
While the student highlights the role of technology in preserving Lo-fi music, a counterperspective on how popularity might dilute artistic authenticity could enrich the evaluation of fragility.
The incentive for human scientists to improve knowledge is asserted. The student should specify who drives this incentive (e.g., institutions, peer review) to ground the claim in real practice.

The progression from Brownâs Decay Theory to Baddeleyâs multi-component model and Jonides et al. demonstrates a clear grasp of how foundational theories evolve, reflecting the Human Sciencesâ self-correcting nature.

The discussion of Boas and Turner is informative, but it lacks explicit connection to the essayâs fragility framework. The student should tie these examples back to how methodological ethics reduce or expose fragility.

The Keynesian versus Friedman case vividly shows theoretical fragility, but the essay should link the policy failures back to the core concept of fragility (e.g., unpredictability, lack of metrics) more explicitly.

The conclusion restates key points effectively but stops short of synthesising their broader implications. The student should discuss what these findings mean for future knowledge creation and maintenance.

After presenting the Gallini and Wilson examples, the student should explicitly link these contrasting interpretations back to the prescribed title to reinforce how they demonstrate the fragility of revered artistic knowledge.

The claim that âArt focuses on expression rather than educative valueâ needs support. The student should explain why this prioritisation inherently undermines the stability and transmissibility of artistic knowledge.

Transitions between the discussion of Solzhenitsyn and Lo-fi music are abrupt. The student should add bridging sentences to clarify why these examples are comparable in illustrating fragility and reverence.
The Akerlof quotation is relevant, but the essay should articulate how established terminologies concretely enhance the reliability and reverence of economic theories.
The sentence ending with a dash suggests an unfinished thought (âââ). The student should complete this sentence to avoid leaving the reader uncertain about the intended point.

The argument that artâs political expression adds fragility introduces an important counterclaim but is not developed. The student should provide a concrete example to illustrate how controversy in art specifically undermines reverence.
The Solzhenitsyn example is compelling but would benefit from deeper exploration of its broader implications for how socioâpolitical shifts can abruptly invert the status of revered knowledge.
The Lo-fi music case study is cut off midâexample. The student should fully develop how subsequent audience participation concretely restored reverence to this genre to complete the argument.

The essay effectively contrasts collective preservation in the Arts with the resistance to fragility in the Human Sciences, demonstrating clear awareness of how community engagement affects knowledge stability.

The link between Khrushchevâs censorship liberalisation and the subsequent fragility of Solzhenitsynâs novel should be clarified. Explain how shifting political paradigms directly translate into knowledge vulnerability.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
